It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking object center moves white diamond there and shifts clicked object along path toward next object. Oscillating between magenta center [56,27] and dark gray diamond [60,27] is the current mechanic.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'purple_ring': 'hollow diamond ring at ~21,39', 'white_diamond': 'white diamond that follows clicks, moves to clicked position', 'gray_path': 'reconfigurable diagonal path', 'purple_square': 'purple square with magenta center at [56,27]', 'dark_gray_diamond': 'dark gray diamond at [60,27]'}
- Win pattern: HYPOTHESIS: Continue oscillating clicks until level completes
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image after clicking dark gray then purple:
- The dark gray diamond moved UP into the ring position! It's now inside the purple ring.
- The purple square is still in the middle
- The white diamond is now between purple square and where dark gray was
So clicking dark gray moved it to the ring. Now I need to click the purple square to move it to the ring too.
The pattern is: click an object → it moves to the ring position.
Let me click the purple square to move it to the ring as well.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Check current state
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c, p = n['color'], n['pixels']
    if c in ('G', 'p', 'W') and p > 5:
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min(x[0] for x in b) + max(x[0] for x in b))//2
        cc = (min(x[1] for x in b) + max(x[1] for x in b))//2
        print(f"{c}: pixels={p}, center=({r},{cc})")

# Dark gray moved to ring! Now click purple square to move it to ring
# Purple square center was at (40, 33)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 33}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking an object moves it to the ring position"}, "object_taxonomy": {"purple_ring": "target at top", "purple_square": "movable object", "dark_gray_diamond": "movable object, now in ring", "white_diamond": "cursor"}, "win_pattern": "Click each object to move it to ring", "level_log": {"level_1": "Click dark gray→ring, need to click purple→ring"}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Dark gray moved to ring. Purple square still needs to move.", "goal_model": "Click purple square to move it to ring", "recent_findings": ["Clicking dark gray moved it to ring position"], "open_questions": ["Does clicking purple move it to ring?"], "current_plan": "Click purple square", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

G: pixels=12, center=(21,39)
p: pixels=20, center=(30,36)
W: pixels=12, center=(40,33)
